response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "I've never been a big fan of social media myself, mostly because I don't spend much time there. But from what I know, the problem of fake content is quite serious out there. You have people spreading misinformation, scams, and all sorts of junk that can really mess things up. Taking screenshots doesn't always help because it's easy to copy them or dismiss them quickly without understanding their impact.\n\nAs someone who has good mental health but isn't particularly tech-savvy due to my low openness score on the Big Five personality test, I think a practical approach is key. One thing that comes to mind is reporting these posts as soon as you see them because it might help reduce visibility over time. But honestly, what's more important for me personally is not engaging with the content at all. If something doesn't look genuine or if someone claims they're raising funds online and I can tell something's off—like inconsistent stories about their lives—I simply don't interact much.\n\nI also find that staying informed through reliable news sources instead of social media feeds gives me peace of mind. Since my conscientiousness is low, I might not dive deep into researching countermeasures or algorithms for fact-checking myself. But looking back on my life experience being a native-born American in New York with three kids and good mental health helps keep things grounded.\n\nAnother idea is to focus more on content you create yourself rather than consuming it all day long. With hobbies like painting, baking, gardening, cinema, and running taking up most of my time now that I'm unemployed but still active around town—I don't really miss the drama online anyway.",

  "tone": "Pragmatic but slightly detached due to limited personal exposure and high extraversion focusing more on real-life interactions instead of online ones",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've never been a big fan of social media myself, mostly because I don't spend much time there. But from what I know, the problem of fake content is quite serious out there. You have people spreading misinformation, scams, and all sorts of junk that can really mess things up. Taking screenshots doesn't always help because it's easy to copy them or dismiss them quickly without understanding their impact.

As someone who has good mental health but isn't particularly tech-savvy due to my low openness score on the Big Five personality test, I think a practical approach is key. One thing that comes to mind is reporting these posts as soon as you see them because it might help reduce visibility over time. But honestly, what's more important for me personally is not engaging with the content at all. If something doesn't look genuine or if someone claims they're raising funds online and I can tell something's off—like inconsistent stories about their lives—I simply don't interact much.

I also find that staying informed through reliable news sources instead of social media feeds gives me peace of mind. Since my conscientiousness is low, I might not dive deep into researching countermeasures or algorithms for fact-checking myself. But looking back on my life experience being a native-born American in New York with three kids and good mental health helps keep things grounded.

Another idea is to focus more on content you create yourself rather than consuming it all day long. With hobbies like painting, baking, gardening, cinema, and running taking up most of my time now that I'm unemployed but still active around town—I don't really miss the drama online anyway.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
I believe fake content is harmful, though I don't spend much time on social media myself. A simple way to deal with it is reporting posts when they're obviously false or suspicious because this might help limit their spread without requiring deep engagement.

Based on my low openness score towards new tech and relatively low conscientiousness in problem-solving complex issues like algorithm design, I prefer straightforward methods that don't involve much technical knowledge.
response_parsed.tone: Pragmatic but slightly detached due to limited personal exposure and high extraversion focusing more on real-life interactions instead of online ones
